content.thinking:
The test passed but there's an unhandled rejection because happy-dom tries to load the script `x.js`. This is a problem — parsing the real `index.html` which contains `` would trigger happy-dom to try to load it, causing unhandled rejections. So using DOMParser with happy-dom to parse the full index.html (which includes a script tag) is problematic. Options: 1. Use a regex/string-based approach to parse the HTML, avoiding DOMParser entirely. 2. Use the `node` environment and use a different parser (not available without extra deps). 3. Use DOMParser but strip the script tag first, or parse with a setting that doesn't load scripts. The cleanest, most robust approach that avoids the unhandled rejection: read the file as a string and use regex-based assertions, OR parse but handle the script issue. Actually, the unhandled rejection happens because happy-dom's DOMParser tries to evaluate/load scripts. Let me think. A string/regex-based approach is actually quite robust and avoids the script-loading side effect. But DOM-based parsing is cleaner for asserting structure (e.g., "noscript is inside body", "noscript comes after #app"). Let me think about a hybrid: I can read the file, then use DOMParser but on a version of the HTML with the script tag removed (or with a harmless src). But that's hacky. Alternatively, I could parse the HTML with DOMParser and the issue is only the external module script. Let me check: the unhandled rejection is from `HTMLScriptElement.#loadScript`. This happens when a script with a `src` is connected to the document. One clean approach: read the HTML, replace the `` with a placeholder like `
`. Then parse. The noscript structure assertions would still work. But this is hacky. Actually, the cleanest robust approach: do string-based assertions. Let me reconsider whether string-based can robustly assert "noscript is inside body and after #app div". Yes: - Find index of ``. - Find index of ``. - Find index of ``. - Assert: bodyStart < noscriptStart < noscriptEnd < bodyEnd. - Assert: appDivIndex < noscriptStart (noscript after #app, per fix instructions). This is clean and robust with regex/String.indexOf. For content checks: - Extract the noscript block content via regex ``. - Assert it contains "JavaScript". - Assert it contains "enable" (enable JavaScript). - Assert it has no interactive controls: regex for `<(button|a|input|form|select|textarea|script)\b` within the block → should be none. For styling visibility: - Within the noscript block, find a `style="..."` attribute. Assert it contains `color`. - To verify visibility on dark bg, I could check the color value. The fix uses `#e6e6e6`. Let me extract the color value and check it's light. But to avoid over-constraining, I'll assert the color value is present and parse it as a light color. Hmm. Actually, let me reconsider. The fix instructions are explicit and give an exact block. The test should verify the fix is applied per instructions. It's reasonable to assert that the noscript has a styled paragraph with a visible color. I'll assert: - The noscript contains a `` parsing in innerHTML context: when scripting is enabled (happy-dom simulates a scripting-enabled environment), `
